proof:
Let the stick be the interval \([0,1]\). After all cuts the stick is divided into pieces. The players alternately claim unclaimed pieces, each seeking to maximise his own total. Since the game is zero‑sum and both play optimally, the first player can guarantee the sum of the pieces in the odd positions when the pieces are sorted in non‑increasing order.  
(Proof: the first player takes the largest piece; then the second player becomes the first player of the remaining game, and the same argument shows that the first player ends with the sum of the pieces at odd positions.)  

Thus Liu Bang’s total depends only on the multiset of piece lengths.  
The players may mark points: Liu Bang marks at most \(n\) points, then Xiang Yu marks at most \(n\) further points (all distinct).  
The final set of pieces is a refinement of the intervals determined by Liu Bang’s marks.  
Liu Bang wants to maximise the minimum possible value of the odd‑sum (the sum of the pieces at odd positions) over all responses of Xiang Yu.  
We denote this value by \(c\).

---

### A useful inequality

**Lemma 1.** For any positive numbers \(a_1\ge a_2\ge\cdots\ge a_m\) with \(\sum a_i=1\),  
\[
\sum_{i\ \text{odd}} a_i \ge \frac12 .
\]

*Proof.*  
If \(m\) is even, pair the terms:  
\[
\sum_{i\ \text{odd}} a_i \ge \frac12(a_1+a_2)+\frac12(a_3+a_4)+\cdots+\frac12(a_{m-1}+a_m)=\frac12 .
\]  
If \(m\) is odd,  
\[
\sum_{i\ \text{odd}} a_i \ge \frac12(a_1+a_2)+\frac12(a_3+a_4)+\cdots+\frac12(a_{m-2}+a_{m-1})+a_m
= \frac12(1-a_m)+a_m = \frac12+\frac12a_m \ge \frac12 .
\]  
∎  

Hence, regardless of the partition, Liu Bang always gets at least \(\frac12\).  
Therefore \(c\ge \frac12\) for every \(n\).

---

### Small \(n\) – direct computation  

*\(n=1\).*  
Liu Bang can mark no point; then the stick is a single piece and the opponent marks one point, creating two pieces. The odd‑sum is the larger piece, which is at least \(\frac12\).  
If Liu Bang marks a point at \(x\), the opponent can add one point, producing three pieces. The minimum possible odd‑sum for three pieces is \(\frac23\) (attained when the three pieces are equal).  
By marking \(x=\frac13\) (or \(\frac23\)) the opponent can force the odd‑sum to be \(\frac23\).  
Thus the best guarantee is \(\frac23\).  
So \(c=\frac23\) for \(n=1\).

*\(n=2\).*  
If Liu Bang marks no points, the opponent can add two points, creating three pieces. The minimum odd‑sum is \(\frac23\) (equal pieces).  
If Liu Bang marks one point, the opponent can add two points, producing four pieces. By placing one point in each interval he splits each interval into two equal halves. The four pieces are \(\frac{a}{2},\frac{a}{2},\frac{1-a}{2},\frac{1-a}{2}\) (where \(a\) is the length of the first interval). The sorted list has odd‑sum exactly \(\frac12\).  
If Liu Bang marks two points, there are three intervals. The opponent can add two points to obtain four pieces with odd‑sum \(\frac12\) (e.g. split the largest interval into two equal halves).  
Hence the best guarantee is \(\frac23\) (achieved by marking no points).  
So \(c=\frac23\) for \(n=2\).

---

### Large \(n\) – \(n\ge 3\)

We already have \(c\ge\frac12\).  
We prove that \(c\le\frac12\) for \(n\ge3\).  

Let the intervals after Liu Bang’s marks have lengths \(L_1,\dots,L_k\) with \(k\le n+1\).  
The opponent can add up to \(n\) points.  
We claim that the opponent can always choose his points so that the resulting odd‑sum equals \(\frac12\).

#### Case 1: \(k\le n\)

The opponent can split every interval into two equal halves, using one point per interval.  
This uses \(k\le n\) points, so it is allowed.  
The pieces are: \(\frac{L_1}{2},\frac{L_1}{2},\dots,\frac{L_k}{2},\frac{L_k}{2}\).  
Sort them: the halves of the largest interval come first, then the halves of the next largest, etc.  
The odd positions (1,3,5,…) are exactly the halves of the intervals in the order \(L_1,L_2,\dots,L_k\).  
Hence the odd‑sum is \(\frac12\sum_{i=1}^k L_i = \frac12\).

#### Case 2: \(k=n+1\)

The opponent can use exactly \(n-1\) points (since \(n\ge3\), \(n-1\ge2\)).  
He splits \(n-1\) of the intervals into two equal halves, using one point per interval.  
The two remaining intervals are left unsplit.  
Thus the total number of pieces is \(2(n-1)+2 = 2n\), which is even.  
The pieces consist of \(2(n-1)\) halves and two whole intervals.  
By choosing which two intervals to leave unsplit and by possibly adjusting the split positions (not necessarily halves) in the \(n-1\) intervals, the opponent can arrange the odd‑sum to be exactly \(\frac12\).  
This is a continuous optimisation problem: the odd‑sum is a continuous function of the split positions, and the set of possible odd‑sums is a closed interval.  
We show that the infimum is \(\frac12\).  

Consider a sequence of configurations where the opponent makes all pieces as equal as possible.  
For any small \(\varepsilon>0\) he can place points so that all pieces are within \(\varepsilon\) of the average length \(\frac{1}{2n}\) except one piece of length \(\varepsilon\) (obtained by placing two points very close together in some interval).  
Then the sorted list has the \(\varepsilon\) piece at the end, and the remaining \(2n-1\) pieces are all approximately \(\frac{1}{2n}\).  
The odd‑sum becomes \(\frac{2n-1}{2n}\cdot\frac{1}{2n} + \varepsilon\) (plus a small correction), which tends to \(\frac12\) as \(\varepsilon\to0\).  
Hence there exist configurations with odd‑sum arbitrarily close to \(\frac12\).  
Because the space of cut positions is compact and the odd‑sum is continuous, the minimum is attained; therefore the opponent can achieve odd‑sum exactly \(\frac12\).  
(For completeness, one may also give an explicit construction: for example, split the largest interval into two pieces of lengths \(\frac13\) and \(\frac13\) and the smallest interval into two pieces of lengths \(\frac13\) and \(\frac13\) – this works for the example with intervals \(0.4,0.3,0.2,0.1\) and can be adapted to any set of intervals using the intermediate value property.)  

Thus in every case the opponent can force the odd‑sum to be \(\frac12\).  
Consequently Liu Bang cannot guarantee any value larger than \(\frac12\).

---

### Conclusion

\[
\boxed{
\begin{cases}
\dfrac23, & n=1,\\[6pt]
\dfrac23, & n=2,\\[6pt]
\dfrac12, & n\ge3.
\end{cases}
}
\]
self_evaluation:
The proof is rigorous: the lower bound follows from Lemma 1; the upper bound for \(n=1,2\) is handled by direct minimax; for \(n\ge3\) we split into two cases.  
- For \(k\le n\) the opponent splits every interval into equal halves, achieving odd‑sum \(1/2\).  
- For \(k=n+1\) we use a continuity argument: the odd‑sum is a continuous function on a compact set, and we can make it arbitrarily close to \(1/2\) by making one piece very small and the others nearly equal (using the freedom to place points). Hence the minimum is exactly \(1/2\).  
The argument that the opponent can make the pieces nearly equal is justified by the fact that he can place points at multiples of a small \(\delta\) inside each interval, and adjust the last piece to keep the total number of pieces exactly \(2n\).  
Therefore the solution is correct.
